title: Keftedes
subtitle: Greek fried meatballs with mint and oregano
excerpt: Keftedes are Greek meatballs of mixed beef and pork, flavoured with mint, oregano, and grated onion, then pan-fried in olive oil until crisp and golden outside and juicy inside. They are eaten as a meze, a main course, or packed cold into a pitta.
sourceNotes: Keftedes are found across Greece and the Balkans in similar forms. The Greek version typically uses a mixture of beef and pork mince with fresh or dried mint, oregano, and soaked bread for texture. They are a constant presence at summer parties and family gatherings.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze the cooked, cooled meatballs on a tray, then transfer to a container. Reheat from frozen in a hot oven for 15 minutes.
recipe.batchNotes: Doubles easily. The raw mixture can be refrigerated overnight.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Form the meatballs and refrigerate for up to 24 hours before frying.

content.text: Keftedes are Greek fried meatballs made from a mixture of beef and pork with mint, oregano, and a little soaked bread to keep them tender. They fry in olive oil until deep golden with a slight crust, and stay juicy inside. The mint is the defining flavour; don't skip it.
Resting the mixture for 30 minutes before shaping helps it hold together during frying.

items.prepNote: grated on the coarse side of a box grater
soaked in 2 tbsp water for 5 minutes

content.text: Combine the beef mince, pork mince, grated onion, grated garlic, soaked breadcrumbs, beaten egg, mint, and oregano in a large bowl. Season well with salt and pepper. Mix with your hands until everything is combined. Cover and rest in the fridge for 30 minutes.
Shape the mixture into balls about the size of a walnut, roughly 3 cm across. Wet your hands lightly between shapes to prevent sticking. You should get about 30 to 35 meatballs.

Heat {{olive-oil}} in a large frying pan over medium-high heat. Fry the meatballs in batches, turning regularly, for 8 to 10 minutes until golden brown all over and cooked through. Don't crowd the pan; leave space between each meatball or they steam rather than fry. Transfer to a plate lined with kitchen paper.

items.symptom: The meatballs break apart in the pan
items.cause: Mixture too wet, or not rested, or handled too early after going in the oil
items.fix: Don't move the meatballs for the first 2 minutes; they need to set a crust before they can be turned. If the mixture seemed very soft, add an extra tablespoon of breadcrumbs.

items.symptom: The meatballs are well-browned outside but pink inside
items.cause: Oil too hot; outside cooked faster than inside
items.fix: Reduce the heat to medium after the initial browning and cook for a minute or two longer per side.

content.text: Keftedes are one of the most beloved Greek meze dishes. They appear at every summer party and family gathering, usually on a plate in the middle of the table, eaten with fingers or with tzatziki and bread. Cold the next day they are equally good in a pitta or alongside a salad.
